inference_unit.problem: Prior to this work there was a clear, application-driven need to identify and experimentally validate hydrogen-rich binary compounds that could realize superconductivity at or near room temperature under high pressure. Ab initio structure-prediction studies had proposed yttrium hydrides (notably fcc-YH$_{10}$, hcp-YH$_{9}$ and bcc-YH$_{6}$) as among the most promising candidates, with predicted critical temperatures (T$_{c}$) exceeding 250 K and, in some calculations, reaching or surpassing room temperature. However, these theoretical predictions were inconsistent with one another: different groups reported conflicting stability ranges and T$_{c}$ estimates (e.g., some studies predicted thermodynamic stability of fcc-YH$_{10}$ at several hundred GPa, while others found it unstable at T=0 K), and anharmonic and other effects were expected to alter T$_{c}$ but were insufficiently constrained. Experimentally, only a few hydride systems had been reproducibly characterized (e.g., H$_3$S, LaH$_{10}$), and yttrium hydrides lacked comprehensive, structure-resolved experimental data: there was no consensus on which Y–H phases actually form under synthesis conditions, what their crystal structures and compositions are, what pressure–temperature windows they occupy and decompose in, and what their superconducting properties (T$_{c}$, isotope effect, upper critical field H$_{c2}$, coherence length $\xi$) are. These gaps prevented a definitive assessment of (i) whether the highest-predicted phases such as fcc-YH$_{10}$ can be synthesized under realistic high-pressure/high-temperature conditions, (ii) the quantitative accuracy of current ab initio predictions for T$_{c}$ and phase stability in the Y–H system, and (iii) whether the superconductivity in synthesized yttrium hydrides is phonon-mediated and shares characteristic electromagnetic parameters with other superhydrides. Because different theoretical works and at least one prior experimental claim for very high T$_{c}$ in Y–H were contradictory and lacked accompanying structural characterization, a systematic experimental study that couples synthesis, X-ray structural identification, electrical transport (including isotope substitution) and magneto-transport under controlled pressures and temperatures was required to resolve which Y–H phases form, to measure their intrinsic superconducting properties, and thereby to test and refine theoretical predictions.

conclusion.title: Synthesis and crystal-structure identification of yttrium hydrides YH3, YH4, YH6 and YH9

conclusions.conclusion:
Yttrium hydrides with nominal compositions YH$_{3}$, YH$_{4}$, YH$_{6}$ and YH$_{9}$ were synthesized in a diamond anvil cell under hydrogen (H$_{2}$ or D$_{2}$) at megabar pressures and, when required, with pulsed-laser heating. Their crystal structures were determined by X-ray powder diffraction and Rietveld/Le Bail refinement and assigned as follows: Fm-3m for YH$_{3}$ (fcc-YH$_{3}$), 14/mmm for YH$_{4}$ (bct-YH$_{4}$), Im-3m for YH$_{6}$ (bcc-YH$_{6}$), and P6$_{3}$/mmc for YH$_{9}$ (hcp-YH$_{9}$). The experimentally measured lattice parameters and volumes as functions of pressure are well described by Vinet equations of state fitted to the data, and the lattice volumes and c/a ratios agree with prior theoretical predictions for these compositions. Independent composition estimates based on the hydrogen-induced volume expansion V$_{H}$ yield approximate hydrogen contents YH$_{5.7(3)}$ and YH$_{8.5(5)}$ for the hydrogen-richer phases assigned to bcc and hcp structures.
In the context of high-pressure hydride research, robust experimental identification of which stoichiometries and crystal structures form under given synthesis conditions is essential because theoretical structure searches produce multiple candidate phases whose relative thermodynamic stability can be sensitive to pressure, temperature and anharmonic contributions. Prior to these measurements, the Y–H system had several mutually incompatible theoretical stability and structural predictions (e.g., differing claims about the stability of fcc-YH$_{10}$ and the pressure ranges for YH$_{9}$ and YH$_{6}$), and there was a lack of direct experimental diffraction data linking specific superconducting signals to well-characterized crystal structures. Without precise structural identification (unit cell symmetry, lattice parameters, volume-pressure relation) and composition estimates, superconducting transitions measured electrically could not be unambiguously assigned to particular Y–H phases, leaving the comparison between theory and experiment unresolved. The present structural assignment and accurate V(P) characterization provide the necessary experimental baseline to map observed superconductivity onto definite crystallographic phases and to evaluate the fidelity of theoretical predictions.
Fig. 1; Fig. 2

conclusion.title: Observation of high-temperature superconductivity in bcc-YH6 and hcp-YH9 with quantified T_c(P)

conclusions.conclusion:
Two synthesized yttrium hydride phases are superconducting: Im-3m (bcc) YH$_{6}$ exhibits superconducting transitions with a dome-shaped pressure dependence and a maximum measured T$_{c}$ of 224 K (measured maximum in reported data) at approximately 166 GPa (with other samples and conditions reporting T$_{c}$ values near 220 K at pressures around 183 GPa), and P6$_{3}$/mmc (hcp) YH$_{9}$ exhibits superconductivity with a dome-shaped pressure dependence and a maximum measured T$_{c}$ of 243 K at 201 GPa. In some samples, transformation of YH$_{6}$ into YH$_{9}$ by laser heating shifts the superconducting transition upward (e.g., from ~211 K for YH$_{6}$ to ~243 K after formation of YH$_{9}$ in the same sample). The T$_{c}$(P) dependences for both phases show initial increase to a maximum and then decreasing T$_{c}$ with further pressure increase.
Within high-pressure superconductivity research, it is crucial to determine whether theoretically predicted high-T$_{c}$ hydride phases actually exhibit superconductivity and what their maximum T$_{c}$ values are under experimentally realizable synthesis conditions. Prior theoretical work had predicted very high T$_{c}$s for several Y–H compositions (including predictions exceeding 300 K for fcc-YH$_{10}$ and 250–276 K for YH$_{6}$/YH$_{9}$), but without comprehensive experimental transport measurements these predictions remained untested. Moreover, when multiple hydride phases can form under similar conditions, attributing an observed electrical transition to a specific crystallographic phase requires simultaneous structural characterization. Before this work, there were contradictory experimental reports and no consensus on which Y–H phases are superconducting and what their intrinsic T$_{c}$(P) maxima and pressure ranges are; thus quantitative, phase-resolved electrical measurements were required to establish the actual superconducting properties of synthesized Y–H phases.
Fig. 4

conclusion.title: Demonstration of a conventional (phonon-mediated) isotope effect with measured isotope coefficients

conclusions.conclusion:
Substituting hydrogen with deuterium in the synthesized hydrides shifts the superconducting transition to lower temperatures: measured T$_{c}$ values for YD$_{6}$ and YD$_{9}$ are lower than their hydrogen counterparts. Quantitatively, using the definition $\alpha = -\frac{d\ln T_{c}}{d\ln M}$ with M the atomic mass, the isotope-effect coefficient is $\alpha \approx 0.39$ for bcc-YH$_{6}$/YD$_{6}$ and $\alpha \approx 0.50$ for hcp-YH$_{9}$/YD$_{9}$ (values obtained from T$_{c}$ measurements in the 183–205 GPa pressure range). The observed isotope shift supports a phonon-assisted (conventional) superconducting pairing mechanism in these yttrium hydrides.
To establish the pairing mechanism in newly discovered high-T$_{c}$ hydride superconductors, isotope-substitution experiments provide a direct test: in a conventional electron–phonon mechanism, replacing H by the heavier D reduces phonon frequencies and lowers T$_{c}$ following an isotope exponent $\alpha$ (classically $\alpha=0.5$ in the harmonic BCS limit). Prior to this study, theoretical treatments of Y–H superconductivity included approximations (harmonic/anharmonic phonons, Migdal–Eliashberg calculations) but lacked experimental isotope data that could validate or falsify the phonon-mediated scenario and constrain anharmonic contributions. Without measured isotope exponents, the role of phonons and anharmonicity in setting T$_{c}$ in yttrium hydrides remained speculative. The measured isotope coefficients reported here thus provide direct empirical evidence toward a conventional phonon-mediated origin and quantify deviations from the harmonic limit for these phases.
Fig. 4

conclusion.title: Upper critical fields, coherence lengths, and estimated Fermi velocities for YH6 and YH9

conclusions.conclusion:
Magneto-transport measurements up to 9 T yield temperature-dependent upper critical fields H$_{c2}$(T) that were fit by Ginzburg–Landau (GL) and Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) models to estimate H$_{c2}$(0 K) and superconducting coherence lengths. For P6$_{3}$/mmc YD$_{9}$ at P$_{H}\approx205\,$GPa the fits give $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0)=60\,$T (GL fit $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{GL}}(0)=46\,$T) and a coherence length $\xi(0)\approx 2.3\text{--}2.7\,$nm using $\xi=\sqrt{\frac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$ with $\phi_{0}$ the magnetic flux quantum. For Im-3m YH$_{6}$ (sample conditions reported) the fits yield $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0)=157\,$T ($H_{c2}^{\mathrm{GL}}(0)=107\,$T) and $\xi(0)\approx 1.45\text{--}1.75\,$nm. For hcp-YH$_{9}$ an estimate $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0)\approx120\,$T was obtained by scaling arguments assuming comparable Fermi velocities in deuteride and hydride counterparts. Using the BCS relation $\xi = 0.18\,\frac{h v_{F}}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{c}}$ and measured $\xi$ and T$_{c}$ values yields estimated Fermi velocities $v_{F}\approx 2.85\times10^{5}\,$m/s for one phase and $\approx2.3\times10^{5}\,$m/s for the other, indicating similar carrier dispersion scales among these superhydrides.
Accurate electromagnetic parameters such as the upper critical field H$_{c2}$, the coherence length $\xi$, and the carrier Fermi velocity $v_{F}$ are essential to characterize the superconducting condensate, to compare different high-T$_{c}$ hydrides, and to test microscopic models (e.g., whether multi-gap behavior or unusual band structure features are implicated). Prior experimental reports on Y–H lacked comprehensive magneto-transport datasets and GL/WHH analyses that would yield H$_{c2}$(0) and $\xi$; theoretical predictions of these quantities depend sensitively on electronic structure details and could not be validated. Without these measured parameters, it was impossible to quantify the robustness of superconductivity against magnetic fields or to relate observed T$_{c}$ differences to electronic dispersion. The present H$_{c2}$ and $\xi$ determinations therefore fill a crucial experimental gap for the Y–H system.
Fig. 5; Fig. 4

conclusion.title: Failure to synthesize fcc‑YH10 under explored conditions; YH9 is the hydrogen-richest observed phase up to 410 GPa and 2250 K

conclusions.conclusion:
Extensive synthesis trials, including laser heating of YH$_{3}$ with NH$_{3}$BH$_{3}$ up to temperatures of 1600–2250 K and pressures up to 410 GPa, did not produce the theoretically predicted fcc-YH$_{10}$ phase. Instead, the hydrogen-richest yttrium hydride observed under the experimental pressure–temperature range was hcp-YH$_{9}$ (P6$_{3}$/mmc), which remained the dominant hydrogen-rich phase even at 410 GPa and 2250(10) K. No diffraction evidence or transient signatures attributable to fcc-YH$_{10}$ were detected during in situ heating or in quenched products within the explored conditions.
Ab initio predictions had identified fcc-YH$_{10}$ as a prime candidate for the highest T$_{c}$ in Y–H, with some computations placing its thermodynamic stability at pressures accessible in experiments (e.g., ~250–400 GPa) and predicting T$_{c}$ values exceeding 300 K. Nonetheless, theoretical results were inconsistent about its T=0 K thermodynamic stability, and some suggested that finite temperature (entropic) effects could stabilize YH$_{10}$ only at high temperatures. Prior to the present work there was no experimental diffraction-based confirmation that fcc-YH$_{10}$ can be synthesized and retained after quenching under realistic laboratory conditions; thus the central question—whether the highest-predicted Y–H superhydride is experimentally accessible at pressures and temperatures reachable with current techniques—remained open. Demonstrating the absence of fcc-YH$_{10}$ and the persistence of YH$_{9}$ under very high P–T conditions therefore directly addresses whether the highest-theory candidate is realizable and constrains theoretical phase diagrams.
Fig. 3

conclusion.title: Measured T_c values for YH6 and YH9 are systematically lower than many prior theoretical predictions

conclusions.conclusion:
The experimentally measured maximum T$_{c}$ values for bcc-YH$_{6}$ (maximum measured ~224 K in these experiments) and hcp-YH$_{9}$ (maximum measured 243 K) are approximately 20–30 K lower than several prior theoretical predictions that reported T$_{c}$ ranges of 251–264 K for YH$_{6}$ and 253–276 K for YH$_{9}$. Further theoretical work that includes effects beyond the approximations used in some prior calculations (e.g., strong anharmonicity, precise electron–phonon coupling calculations, finite-temperature free-energy differences) is required because existing anharmonic-corrected calculations for YH$_{6}$ still predict T$_{c}$ values higher than those experimentally observed.
The predictive power of ab initio superconductivity calculations for hydrides is central to targeted materials discovery, yet their quantitative accuracy depends on the treatment of phonons (harmonic vs anharmonic), electron–phonon matrix elements, and the choice of approximations (Migdal–Eliashberg, static screening, etc.). Prior to this work, theory predicted higher T$_{c}$s for YH$_{6}$ and YH$_{9}$ than experimentally documented in other systems, but direct, phase-resolved experimental T$_{c}$ measurements were missing to benchmark and refine these methods. Without such benchmarks, it was unclear whether discrepancies stem from computational approximations or from differences between predicted and actually formed phases. The observed systematic shortfall of experiment relative to many predictions therefore indicates that theoretical treatments require refinement and provides quantitative targets for theory improvement.

conclusion.title: Experimental stability ranges and decomposition pressures for synthesized Y–H phases

conclusions.conclusion:
The high-hydrogen-content phases have the following experimentally observed stability/decomposition behaviour on decompression: hcp-YH$_{9}$ decomposes into bcc-YH$_{6}$ upon pressure reduction at $\sim159\,$GPa in the reported sample; bcc-YH$_{6}$ decomposes at $\sim135\,$GPa in a deuterated sample; bct-YH$_{4}$ remains stable down to at least $\sim135\,$GPa. These experimental decomposition pressures are higher than some theoretical equilibrium formation pressures predicted in prior works (for example, theoretical equilibrium formation pressure for YH$_{9}$ was predicted near 100 GPa by some calculations), indicating significant hysteresis and possible kinetic/thermodynamic differences between experiment and different theoretical predictions.
Reliable knowledge of the pressure ranges over which particular hydride stoichiometries are stable is essential both for synthetic strategies (which P–T paths can produce and retain a phase) and for testing theoretical phase diagrams. Before this work, ab initio convex-hull and phonon-stability calculations proposed various formation pressures for YH$_{9}$, YH$_{6}$ and related phases, but experimental determination of decomposition pressures and the presence of large baric hysteresis were lacking. Because experiments frequently access phases via kinetically assisted pathways and because experimental equilibrium pressures are often closer to decomposition pressures, direct measurement of the pressures at which Y–H phases decompose provides a necessary empirical map to compare with theory and to guide future synthesis attempts.
Fig. 2; Fig. 4

conclusion.title: Absence of superconductivity in fcc-YH3 and bct-YH4 within measured T ranges contradicts some theoretical predictions

conclusions.conclusion:
Electrical resistance measurements found no superconducting transition in fcc-YH$_{3}$ down to 5 K for pressures in the 15–180 GPa range, and no superconductivity was observed in bct-YH$_{4}$ down to 78 K at 250 GPa. These negative results contrast with some theoretical predictions that had assigned superconducting T$_{c}$ values of ~40 K for YH$_{3}$ and 84–95 K for YH$_{4}$.
Theoretical studies had predicted appreciable T$_{c}$ values for lower-hydrogen-content yttrium hydrides (e.g., YH$_{3}$, YH$_{4}$), suggesting they might also exhibit measurable superconductivity under accessible pressures. Prior to experimental confirmation, it was unknown whether those lower hydrides would superconduct at experimentally accessible temperatures or whether their predicted T$_{c}$s were overestimated due to theoretical approximations. Demonstrating the absence of superconductivity in these phases within the tested temperature windows provides an important constraint on theoretical models and clarifies which stoichiometries are responsible for high-T$_{c}$ behaviour in the Y–H system.
Supplementary Fig. 4; Fig. 2

conclusion.title: Measured T_c(P) profiles have dome-like shapes with maxima and subsequent decreases at higher pressures

conclusions.conclusion:
For both Im-3m YH$_{6}$ and P6$_{3}$/mmc YH$_{9}$ the dependence of T$_{c}$ on pressure exhibits a dome-like form: T$_{c}$ rises with pressure to a maximum (YH$_{6}$ maximum ~224 K near 166 GPa; YH$_{9}$ maximum 243 K at 201 GPa) and then decreases with further increasing pressure. The decrease in T$_{c}$ at higher pressures is consistent with pressure-induced phonon stiffening or other electronic-structure changes; sharp drops in T$_{c}$ below certain pressures correspond to structural distortions and phase transformations to lower-symmetry phases.
The pressure dependence of T$_{c}$ contains key information about the interplay between lattice dynamics, electron–phonon coupling and electronic structure. Before these experiments, theoretical studies provided pressure-dependent T$_{c}$ predictions but lacked comprehensive experimental maps of T$_{c}$(P) for Y–H phases. Without experimental T$_{c}$(P) curves, it was not possible to identify pressure regions of optimal superconductivity, to relate maxima to structural instabilities, or to test hypotheses about phonon stiffening or multi-gap behaviour under compression. Establishing dome-like T$_{c}$(P) profiles for YH$_{6}$ and YH$_{9}$ therefore supplies critical empirical guidance for interpreting mechanisms and for targeting pressure conditions that maximize T$_{c}$.
Fig. 4

inference_unit.open_question: Several experimentally and scientifically meaningful issues remain unresolved and were explicitly identified by the authors: (1) The quantitative origin of the systematic discrepancy between measured T$_{c}$ values (for YH$_{6}$ and YH$_{9}$) and many prior theoretical predictions remains unresolved — existing anharmonic corrections reduce but do not eliminate the gap, so improved theoretical treatments (better anharmonicity, electron–phonon coupling, finite-temperature free energies, or beyond-Migdal effects) are required. (2) The reason why fcc‑YH$_{10}$, predicted by some calculations to be thermodynamically favorable at high pressure and temperature, was not observed under experimental conditions up to 410 GPa and 2250 K is unresolved; it remains an open question whether fcc‑YH$_{10}$ can be stabilized at even higher P–T conditions, whether kinetic barriers prevent its formation, or whether theoretical stability estimates are incorrect. (3) The anomalous relation between T$_{c}$ and H$_{c2}$ observed for YH$_{9}$ versus YH$_{6}$ (i.e., higher T$_{c}$ but lower H$_{c2}$ in hcp-YH$_{9}$ compared with bcc-YH$_{6}$) lacks an ab initio explanation and calls for further electronic-structure and superconducting-gap calculations to determine whether multi-band effects, Fermi-surface topology, or other band-structure details produce this behaviour. (4) Contradictions between different experimental reports (notably the disagreement with the report claiming T$_{c}=262\,$K in Ref. 27) remain unresolved because those prior works lack concomitant structural characterization; reconciling different experimental claims requires further phase-resolved measurements and cross-validation. (5) Some impurity or unidentified phases with complex diffraction patterns and uncertain stoichiometry were observed in samples with hydrogen deficiency; their compositions, structures and possible electronic properties are still unknown. Addressing these open issues requires coordinated advances in higher‑fidelity theoretical modelling, targeted high-P–T synthesis experiments (possibly at still higher pressures/temperatures), and further phase-resolved structural and spectroscopic characterization.
